videos.title: Life in ESTONIA - The Country of LONELY WOMEN and ATHEISTS - DOCUMENTARY VLOG
videos.description:
Estonia is a small, tech-savvy country in Northern Europe with a population of 1.4 million. Its capital, Tallinn, is home to a third of the population. Known for forests, over 1,500 islands, and cold weather, Estonia is part of the EU and uses the Euro. It's highly digital citizens vote, pay taxes, and even apply for residency online. Tallinn offers free public transport, and internet is fast and free nationwide. Jobs in IT pay well, while average wages range from €1,600 to €10,000. Life is quiet, safe, and efficient, but locals can be reserved. Estonians are highly educated, mostly atheist, and many speak Estonian, Russian, and English. Renting costs €400 to €1,500 monthly, and groceries average €300. Digital residency attracts global talent due to low birth rates. Overall, Estonia is peaceful, modern, and ideal for skilled workers and digital nomads.

videos.transcript: Estonia, officially known as the Republic of Estonia, is a small Baltic nation in Northern Europe. With a population of around 1.4 million people, it is one of the least populated countries in Europe. The capital city is Talon. And interestingly, about 1/3 of the entire population lives there. Nearly half of Estonia's land is covered in dense forests, and these are strictly protected. No one is allowed to cut them down. Besides its mainland, Estonia also has over 1,500 islands. However, because of its cold northern location, most of these islands are uninhabited. This is quite different from Greece, where almost every island is settled and marketed for tourism. Today the country is known for its techsavvy society and is often nicknamed the internet republic due to its advanced digital infrastructure. Estonia is a democratic parliamentary republic and has been a member of the European Union since 2011, becoming its 17th member. Thanks to EU membership, Estonians now enjoy free movement across Europe. This freedom to travel and work across the continent has significantly boosted the quality of life in Estonia. People's overall happiness has improved. And with the adoption of the euro, the country's economy has also seen steady growth. If you're thinking about visiting Estonia, you better be prepared for cold weather. The country's climate is heavily influenced by the Baltic Sea, which means even in summer, temperatures rarely go above 17° C, 62° F. In winter, it's not uncommon for temperatures to drop to - 10° C, 14° F. Snow and cold dominate most of the year, giving the country a perpetual winter feel. So, if you're planning a trip to Estonia, I'd recommend going in the spring or summer months. Otherwise, you might find yourself stuck indoors, escaping the cold wind and constant rain. Also, Estonians who have lived in the cold for many years often find it quite challenging to adapt when visiting warmer countries like Turkey. Traveling to Estonia is fairly straightforward. From Istanbul, you can either take a direct flight to Talon, which takes about 3 hours, or choose a connecting flight that could take 5 to 6 hours depending on your route. Of course, you'll need a Shenhen visa just like you would for any other EU country. If we take a look at everyday life in Estonia, one of the first things to mention is the minimum wage, which is currently around €886. Compared to most European countries, that's on the lower side. But here's the thing. What really matters isn't just how much you earn, but what you can actually buy with that money. Besides, in most European countries, very few people actually earn the minimum wage. Anyway, in Estonia, the average salaries generally range from €1,600 up to €10,000. But of course, those earning €8,000 to€10,000 are usually top level executives. So your salary really depends on what kind of job you do. For example, manual laborers in Estonia typically earn about €1,100 a month, while software developers and IT engineers make between €3,000 and €7,000 plus. Teachers, on the other hand, earn somewhere around €1,500 to €2,000. People usually work 40 hours a week, spread over 58-hour days. And for those over 18 who are unemployed, the Estonian government provides about €300 per month as unemployment support. In my opinion, if you have skills in computers or software development, Estonia should be at the top of your list of countries to work in. Estonia is one of the most techsavvy countries in the world, especially when it comes to software and IT. Also, since most jobs here follow a 5-day work week, you might want to look for additional ways to earn extra income. For example, just like in the US, you can make good money by driving for Uber in Estonia. Of course, you'll need to have a car for that. Alternatively, you could work part-time as a courier or pick up shifts as a waiter on weekends. But honestly, if your goal isn't to save up a lot of money and you just want to enjoy life, having one regular job is enough to live comfortably here. You won't starve in Estonia, my friends. When you arrive in Estonia, you'll notice that the official language is Estonian. Since Estonian is closely related to Finnish, people from Finland and Estonia can understand each other quite easily. During the long Soviet era, Russian was the dominant language, so you'll also hear a lot of Russian spoken, especially among older generations. Besides that, English and German are also commonly spoken minority languages. The multilingual environment is a good indicator of Estonia's high education levels. In fact, Estonia holds the world record for the highest literacy rate. Of course, given the country's small population, it's not entirely surprising that the percentage is so high. When it comes to religion, around 80% of Estonians don't actively follow any faith. Estonia is considered the most atheist country in the world by percentage. Following Estonia in this regard are countries like the Czech Republic and Sweden. Only about 16% of Estonians say they believe in God. There's even a joke that goes, "You might go to Estonia as a believer, but you'll leave as a non-believer." For example, even though Estonians accepted Christianity centuries ago, it's still surprising how they never fully embraced it. Those who are Christian often follow Western denominations, while the Russian minority tends to practice Orthodox Christianity. There's also a tiny Muslim community of around 3,000 people, mainly of Tatar and Azerbaijani descent. When you walk through Estonian streets after 8:00 p.m., you'll notice how quiet and empty they get. But don't let that give you the wrong impression about safety. Estonia is actually one of the safest countries in Europe when it comes to serious crimes like harassment, assault, incest, or fraud. And if you're into night life, don't worry. The country has plenty of bars and clubs to keep you entertained after dark. Interestingly, there are more women than men in Estonia, and women tend to live about 10 years longer. In fact, Estonia ranks number one in Europe in terms of women per man, even surpassing Lithuania. On top of that, some of the world's most stunning supermodels come from Estonia. Their genetic line has remained mostly untouched, and thanks to historical ties with Finland, the pure beauty of Northern Europe is deeply embedded in Estonian women. Most Estonian women are tall, blonde, and have strikingly colorful eyes, and they certainly know how to make the most of these features. Instead of spending years on stressful jobs or long academic careers, many Estonian girls pursue modeling as soon as they reach adulthood. This way, they manage to achieve their dreams within just a few years, leading lives that are far less stressful and much happier compared to men. But there's something important to know. Estonians, much like their country, are quite reserved and cautious when it comes to strangers. For instance, in the US, strangers might smile and greet you on the street. In Northern Europe, though, people often judge by appearances. If you're visiting Estonia with dark hair, dark eyes, and darker features, you'll likely experience a cold reception at first. There's still a prevailing mindset that people from the Middle East, Africa, and parts of Asia are not as civilized or trustworthy. So, if you're thinking of settling down in Estonia, you'll need to invest quite a bit of time to build social connections. Now, let's talk about one of Estonia's coolest features, the internet. If you're someone who spends most of your day online, Estonia's internet infrastructure will blow your mind. Internet freedom here is absolute and with an average speed of 41 megabits per second, Estonia has one of the fastest internet networks in the world. They've taken full advantage of the digital age and are easily 20 years ahead of many countries in terms of digital infrastructure. You can access free Wi-Fi almost anywhere in Estonia. In fact, their constitution literally states that internet access is a basic human right. No matter who comes to power, this service is guaranteed for everyone. You might wonder how such a small country achieved what many larger nations couldn't. The secret lies in Estonia's size. The entire country spans just 45,228 km. That's about 17 times smaller than Turkey. Because of its compact geography, Estonia has been able to establish lightning fast internet and efficient transportation networks. Everything from paying taxes to voting in elections is done online. For instance, if you're an Estonian citizen living abroad, you don't need to fly back home just to vote. As long as you have internet access, you can participate in national decisions in a matter of seconds. Estonia has moved almost every aspect of life into the digital space, making everyday tasks incredibly simple and efficient. Since the year 2000, Estonia has gone completely paperless for government procedures. All legal processes are handled electronically. Even in schools, kids as young as seven are given computer and coding lessons. Another clever innovation from Estonia is their digital residency program. If you want to open a company or get a residency permit in Estonia, you can apply online without paying hefty fees to consultancy firms. Through this digital system, Estonia aims to increase its population to 10 million by 2025. Why? Because the country's birth rates are low, and with only 1.5 million people, Estonia's future economic stability is uncertain, they need more people to fuel productivity and boost development. By welcoming skilled individuals through digital applications, Estonia not only gains talent, but also collects taxes from these new digital residents. In fact, this system has become a significant revenue stream for the government. Travel within Estonia is also incredibly convenient. Much like in Scandinavian countries, you can get from one city to another in under an hour. This saves a lot of time for locals. The absence of traffic problems is another bonus thanks to the wellestablished tram networks in city centers. These trams let you hop around cities without dealing with buses. Speaking of buses, public transport in the capital, Talon, is completely free. And this easy transportation system doesn't just benefit Estonians. It's a huge plus for their Finnish neighbors, too. Since Finland is right above Estonia, many Finns take a quick ferry ride over to stock up on basic goods. As life in Finland is significantly more expensive, Estonia holds the distinction of being the first nation to break away from the Soviet Union and print its own currency. The economy primarily relies on petroleum and industry, but fishing is still a common livelihood for some. Due to the land not being very suitable for agriculture, rural populations focus more on livestock, particularly cattle and pigs. In short, Estonia is one of the most peaceful and fascinating countries in the world. A hidden gem definitely worth visiting. Now, if you're planning to move to Estonia by yourself, renting a studio or a one-bedroom apartment in the capital, Talon, will cost you anywhere between 400 and 500 per month. But if you're thinking of moving there with your family, be prepared to spend at least €800 for a bigger place. Estonia's population is quite small, so families tend to be small as well. And developers don't often build large apartments. That's why larger apartments in city centers are rare and can go up to 1,500 in rent. Houses in Estonia are categorized into A, B, and C classes with Aclass homes being the most comfortable. For instance, even in freezing cold weather, an A-class home will have excellent insulation, saving you a lot on energy bills. But naturally, the more luxurious the apartment the higher the rent. On the other hand, if you live in a lowerass home, you'll probably need to keep the heating on all year round, which can become a big hassle. For a single person, monthly grocery expenses, including meat, vegetables, and other essentials, usually amount to around €250 to €300. On top of that, utilities like electricity, internet, and gas will add an extra €100 to your monthly bills. Estonians generally enjoy their wine, and you'll find that nearly every home has at least one bottle. A standard 700ml bottle of wine in a supermarket costs around 15 to20. If you plan to go out for an evening, including food and drinks, expect to spend around €30. In their free time, people in Estonia often hit the gym or enjoy winter sports. For men, getting a haircut can be a bit of a problem, though. Barbers typically charge between 30 and40 for a basic haircut, and that doesn't even include a beard trim. Honestly, buying a hair clipper and cutting your own hair might be a smarter choice here. Of course, there are always people who try to work under the table, but employers in Estonia rarely take that risk. The fine for hiring an undocumented worker is about €1,500 per person. On top of that, the worker can face hefty fines and even be banned from entering Estonia for up to 10 years. If you want to work legally in Estonia, you need to apply for a type E work visa through the Estonian embassy in Anchora or its local consulates. This brings us to the end of the video. See you in the next one. Goodbye.

videos.title: TALLINN in Just 4 HOURS | Guide to the Capital of Estonia
videos.description:
Tallinn is a lovely city with a UNESCO World Heritage Site old town that's amazingly well preserved. It's also a city that many people just visit for short trips. This is a guide for how to maximize your visit to Tallinn...if you only have four hours to spare!

For more videos every week, subscribe to the channel: https://www.youtube.com/threestarvagabond?sub_confirmation=1

Also, join the Three Star Vagabond Facebook group to discuss upcoming videos: https://www.facebook.com/groups/threestarvagabond

This video covers some basic history of Tallinn, dating back to the 13th century and the Hanseatic League, and then gives you suggestions for where you can go if you just have one day or even half a day in the city.

The places mentioned or shown include:

- The Town Hall Square, with the Raeapteek (Europe's oldest pharmacy) and Old Thomas
- Tallinn Old Town, including St Catherine's Passage, the Kohtuotsa Viewing Platform, and the Patkuli Vieweing Platform
- Other things to keep an eye out for in Tallinn Old Town, such as street art, the Lucky Chimney Sweeper, the Chopin Bench, and the Black Angus sculpture
- Kiek in the Kök, a famous tower turned museum
- The Seaplane Harbor Museum
- The Rock of Tondi, as made famous by vlogger Bald and Bankrupt
- Gorgeous Kadriorg Palace and Kadriorg Park

There's of course a lot more to see in Tallinn! But if you have a short amount of time, all of this will keep you plenty occupied.

videos.description:
Tallinn Travel Guide 2025 - Best Places to Visit in Tallinn Estonia in 2025

Tallinn, Estonia’s capital on the Baltic Sea, is the country’s cultural hub. It retains its walled, cobblestoned Old Town, home to cafes and shops, as well as Kiek in de Kök, a 15th-century defensive tower. Its Gothic Town Hall, built in the 13th century and with a 64m-high tower, sits in historic Tallinn’s main square. St. Nicholas Church is a 13th-century landmark exhibiting ecclesiastical art.

Estonia, a country in Northern Europe, borders the Baltic Sea and Gulf of Finland. Including more than 1,500 islands, its diverse terrain spans rocky beaches, old-growth forest and many lakes. Formerly part of the Soviet Union, it's dotted with castles, churches and hilltop fortresses. The capital, Tallinn, is known for its preserved Old Town, museums and the 314m-high Tallinn TV Tower, which has an observation deck.

be paid if the tower was finished a number of Builders took up the challenge but all of them fell to their death it became clear that the risk wasn't worth it and so no new volunteers came forward that was until a strange Foreigner named Olaf all he asked was a th000 pieces of gold all the money the city had left Talon had no choice but to accept and so Olaf started to build he worked hard and fast and he made a Spire as high as was requested only one thing was missing the Golden Rooster or in some versions The Golden Cross to be placed on top of the tower Olaf climbed up as the crowd horror as a snake and a toad crawled out of his mouth leading people to Proclaim that the tower was the devil's work and the tower has long been associated with bad luck lightning is known to have videos.transcript: Welcome to a new journey. In this episode, we'll be visiting one of the most beautiful and wellpreserved medieval cities in Europe, [snorts] the capital of Estonia, which boasts a stunning old town declared a UNESCO World Heritage site. Here you'll find charming squares, colorful houses, quaint cafes, and breathtaking [music] viewpoints. Let's take a look at the 10 must-see places in Talin. Number 10, Old Town. The old town of Talin, surrounded by a wall that is still largely intact, is one of the best preserved historic centers in Europe, which is why it has been declared a UNESCO World Heritage [music] site. Strolling through its cobbled streets allows you to imagine what life was like centuries ago, with houses featuring sloping roofs, defensive towers, Gothic churches, and cobblestone squares that seem frozen in time. The heart of the old town is Town Hall Square, a spacious area surrounded by colorful facades of old guild buildings, cafes with terraces, and craft shops. On one side stands Talon Town Hall, a gem [music] of civil Gothic architecture dating from the 15th century, whose tower offers spectacular views of the city. Directly across you'll find Rayuptique, one of the oldestarmacies in the world still in operation, which has been providing remedies since the 15th century [music] and houses an exhibition on ancient medical practices. To learn more about the city's history and not miss its most iconic spots, we recommend booking a guided tour of Talon. I will be leaving the link in the description. Number nine, Craftsman's Courtyard. Located in a small hidden passage in the heart of the old town, the craftsman's courtyard encapsulates the creative and traditional spirit of the city. This courtyard, also known as the master's courtyard or mistrite hoove in Estonian, houses small workshops and shops run by local artisans. [music] Here, visitors can watch the craftsmen in action, carving, molding, or hand painting their creations, making it the perfect spot to take home a unique souvenir from Talin. The surroundings also add to the charm of the place. Surrounded by medieval buildings and decorated with flowers, lanterns, and wooden benches, the courtyard invites visitors to linger for a while or enjoy a coffee at one of the small local cafes. [music] Number eight, St. Catherine's Passage. Another must-see corner in Talin is this narrow alley, which stands out not only for its perfectly preserved medieval atmosphere, but also for the peaceful aura it offers amid the bustling historic center. Walking through this passage is like stepping back several centuries in time. The stone arches, the walls of ancient monasteries, and the windows with rot iron bars surround visitors in a unique atmosphere. One of the distinctive features of St. Catherine's passage is that fragments of the old Dominican monastery that existed in this area during the Middle Ages are still preserved in its walls. Many consider this passage to be one of the most photogenic in the city. Perfect for leisurely wandering, admiring the architectural details and being surprised by every corner. Number seven, Talon Television Tower. The Talon TV tower is one of the most iconic landmarks in Estonia's capital. Standing at a height of 314 m, it is the tallest [music] structure in the country. Its observation deck, located at 170 m, offers breathtaking panoramic views of the city and its surroundings. Inside the tower, multimedia exhibitions narrate its history and its pivotal role during the events of 1991 when it became a strategic point in Estonia's struggle for independence. On August 20th, 1991, the Supreme Council of the Republic of Estonia declared the restoration of the country's independence. The following day, Soviet landing troops attempted to seize the TV tower. People from all over Estonia gathered to defend it, preventing the takeover and turning the tower into a symbol of national resistance and sovereignty. Additionally, the tower houses a rotating restaurant where visitors can enjoy local cuisine, while the landscape slowly changes around them. Given its status as one of the most visited monuments in the city, long lines can form at the ticket counter. To avoid waiting, we recommend purchasing your tickets online. You can find the link in the description. Number six, Tia Hill. This limestone promontory has been inhabited since ancient times and its elevated position made it a strategic location from which the entire region was controlled. At the top of the hill stands Tempia Castle, a medieval fortress that has been transformed over the centuries and now houses the Parliament of Estonia. The combination of ancient [music] defensive walls with more modern structures perfectly reflects the fusion of past and present that characterizes this place. In front of the castle stands the iconic Pik Herman Tower from which the Estonian flag flies. The cobbled streets, colorful facades, and viewpoints offering spectacular views of Talin's old town in the Baltic Sea make this hill an essential place for any visitor. Tia is also home to important religious institutions such as St. Mary's Cathedral and the Alexander Nevki Cathedral, which we will explore next. Number five, Alexander Nevki Cathedral. We remain at the top of Tumpia Hill where we find this Orthodox cathedral built between 1894 and 1900 during the period when Estonia was part of the Russian Empire. It is one of the most recognizable and photographed monuments in the city. Standing out for its stunning neo-Russian architectural style and its iconic onion-shaped domes, [music] the interior is richly decorated with religious icons, mosaics, and golden details, creating a sense of grandeur and reverence. In addition to its aesthetic and religious value, the cathedral holds significant historical meaning. It was built during a time when Russian power sought to assert its influence in the Baltic territories. And during Estonia's 20th century period of independence, its demolition was considered. However, over time, the cathedral was accepted as an integral part of the city's cultural heritage, and today it is preserved and valued as a reflection of Talin's complex and [music] rich history. Number four, the KGB cells located in the city center in what seems to be an ordinary building. These cells were used by the Soviet State Security Committee during the occupation of Estonia to interrogate, imprison, and often torture those considered enemies of the regime. Now converted into a museum, the former KGB headquarters retain several original cells where prisoners were confined under inhumane conditions. Through informative exhibits, personal objects, declassified documents, and testimonies from survivors, the museum reconstructs life under Soviet control, and shows how the repressive apparatus of the KGB operated in Estonia. One of the most moving aspects of the tour, is the fact that many of the prisoners were ordinary citizens, people like you or me, unjustly accused for holding different opinions. [music] In the description, I will be leaving the link for you to purchase tickets. Number three, Viru Gate. The Viru Gate is one of the most iconic entrances to Talin's old town and a visual symbol that welcomes visitors into the medieval heart of the city. This gate is part of the ancient defensive walls that once surrounded Talin. And although the fortified system was much more extensive during its peak, today the Viru gate remains one of the most representative structures from that era. It consists of two round towers topped with pointed roofs and passing under its arch leads directly to Viru Street, one of the busiest in the old town, filled with craft shops, cafes, restaurants, and flowers. Lots of flowers. [music] In fact, one of the most characteristic images of the place is that of flower stalls lined up right next to the gate, adding color and life to the historic surroundings. Number two, P Street. Pick Street, [music] which means long in Estonian, is one of the oldest and most representative streets in Talon's historic center. In the past, this street was the city's main commercial artery, connecting the port with Town Hall Square. Its historical significance is reflected in the number of well-preserved buildings that line its path. Among the most notable buildings on the street are the small guild halls, places where the city's most influential artisans and merchants gathered [music] to discuss business matters and regulate their activities. Also worth admiring is the House of the Brotherhood of the Blackheads, recognizable by its ornate facade and its history linked to unmarried [music] foreign merchants. As you walk along Pik Street, it's easy to notice how each section reveals a different architectural detail from carved wooden gates to ancient emblems and Gothic windows. Number one, walls. One of the most distinctive features of Talin is undoubtedly its city walls. One of the reasons why this city is considered one of the best preserved medieval cities in Europe. Built in the 13th century and expanded in subsequent centuries, these imposing fortifications originally surrounded the entire city [music] to protect it from invasions. Although only a part of the original structure remains today, what is still standing gives a clear idea of the defensive nature Talin had in the Middle Ages. Along the walls, several watchtowers are still standing. These towers not only served as watch posts but also as strategic points to launch projectiles [music] against potential attackers. Today, some of them have been transformed into museums where visitors can learn more about military history and [music] everyday life in the medieval city. One of the most recommended experiences is to walk along the accessible sections of the wall. From there, you get privileged views of the old town with its red rooftops, cobbled streets, and church towers rising above the buildings. videos.transcript: If you are visiting the Estonian capital of Tallinn for the very first time, be sure to watch this video because in it, I'm going to be sharing everything you need to know to have the best trip ever. Now, in case you're new here, hi, I'm Christina from HappyToWander.com, and this channel is all about practical travel advice from someone who's been there, done that, so be sure to like and subscribe if that sounds like your kind of thing. I've personally been to Tallinn twice now and honestly, it is such an underrated city and I'm really, really excited for you to discover it for yourself. Now, let's start with some Tallinn travel planning tips. In terms of where to stay, do aim to stay in or at least very near to the Tallinn Old Town for the most pleasant experience. Outside of the Old Town area, I do have to say there doesn't seem to be a ton to do for tourists. And so I think staying close to this area really makes sense, especially if you want to be able to walk to most of the main sites of interest. My first visit to Tallinn, the Citybox Tallinn, was actually really perfect. It's not in the Old Town, but really walkable to both the Old Town and also the ferry terminal, so I can definitely recommend that if you don't want anything fancy and don't mind maybe a smaller room. Now, in terms of when to visit, there are two main peak visiting times in Tallinn. First off, the summer season and, of course, Christmas season. Summer in Tallinn does give you the best chance at warm weather and Christmas is just straight up magical in this city with some of the most atmospheric Christmas markets in Europe. But of course, this is when crowds are going to be at their highest, bearing in mind that Tallinn is actually quite a popular cruise destination as well. That said, I do have to say Tallinn's peak season crowds are honestly nothing like you'll see in other major tourist hotspots across Europe, so even visiting in peak season is very manageable, but do time your visit for spring or fall for more pleasant temperatures, fewer crowds, and of course, lower prices. With all this in mind, do be aware that June 23rd to 24th are major holidays in Estonia, and so opening hours might have some changes. And how do I know this? Actually, these happen to be the dates of my first trip, very coincidentally, which actually forced me to amend my itinerary a little bit, but honestly, I did not mind. It was 100% worth it because I actually got to experience my very first Estonian Midsummer, which is usually celebrated on Midsummer Eve, being June 23rd. I went to a very fun traditional celebration over at the Estonian Open Air Museum, and honestly, it was so wholesome, even though the weather wasn't the best. So, if you're looking for a very unique cultural experience, I can really recommend visiting during that time. Now, in terms of how long to stay, 3 days is definitely a good amount of time to cover the main sites and attractions in Tallinn. Tallinn's Old Town and its main touristic core, honestly, it might be small, but it is absolutely jam-packed with museums and very interesting attractions and sites. So, if you want to explore in-depth, I would say 3 days makes sense.
